subquestions.question_text: Which description best matches the scatterplot for minutes played and points scored?

options.label: A
options.text: The points form a strong negative linear association.

options.label: B
options.text: The points form a moderate positive linear association.

options.label: D
options.text: The points form a perfect positive line.

subquestions.question_text: How should the association between minutes played and points scored be described?
Find the equation of the hand-drawn best-fit line through \((20,11)\) and \((25,20)\).
Using \(y=\frac95x-25\), predict the score for a player who played 16 minutes.
Calculate Pearson's correlation coefficient for experience and weekly salary.
Calculate Spearman's correlation coefficient after ranking the observations.
Find the regression equation for weekly salary on months of experience.
Use the regression equation to predict the salary for 25 months of experience.
If the actual salary is \(\$950\), calculate the percentage error of the prediction.
Calculate Pearson's correlation coefficient between distance travelled and temperature.
Find the mean distance and mean temperature.
Using \(y=-8.43x+317.93\), estimate Jack's total distance when the temperature is \(21^\circ\text{C}\) and he also walks 15 km.
If the actual distance was \(175\text{ km}\), find the percentage error in the estimate.
Calculate Pearson's correlation coefficient for studying time and SAT score.
Find the regression equation for SAT score on studying time.
Interpret the value of the gradient \(7.40\) in context.

options.label: A
options.text: Each extra point increases studying time by \(7.40\) weeks.

options.label: C
options.text: Each extra week of studying increases the predicted score by about \(7.40\) points.

subquestions.question_text: Predict the SAT score for a student who studied for 40 weeks.
Is it reasonable to predict the score for a student who studied for 3 weeks?

options.label: A
options.text: Yes, because regression always works outside the data range.

options.label: B
options.text: No, because \(3\) weeks is the mean.

options.label: C
options.text: Yes, because the correlation is positive.

options.label: D
options.text: No, because \(3\) weeks is outside the data range.

subquestions.question_text: Calculate Pearson's correlation coefficient for age and 100 m time.
Find the regression equation of time \(y\) on age \(x\).
Find the regression equation of age \(x\) on time \(y\).
Use the regression equation \(x=4.33y-32.50\) to estimate the age of a runner with time \(13.7\) seconds.
Find the mean advertising money spent.
Find the mean sales revenue.
Calculate Pearson's correlation coefficient between advertising money and sales revenue.
Calculate Spearman's rank correlation coefficient for the ranked advertising and revenue data.
Find the regression equation for sales revenue on advertising money.
By how much does each additional dollar spent on advertising increase predicted sales revenue?
Show the predicted revenue for \(\$850\) spent on advertising.
Estimate the sales revenue for a company that spent \(\$930\) on advertising.
Find the regression equations for boys and girls.
Which group benefits more from an additional hour of studying?

options.label: A
options.text: Boys, because their intercept is greater.

options.label: C
options.text: Girls, because their gradient \(5.23\) is greater than \(3.09\).

subquestions.question_text: Find the intersection point of the two regression equations.
Find the mean height of the athletes.
Find the mean jump length.
Find the regression line of jump length on height.
Find the x-intercept of \(y=6.71x-557.45\).
Find the y-intercept of \(y=6.71x-557.45\).
Interpret the gradient \(6.71\) in context.

options.label: A
options.text: For each extra \(1\text{ cm}\) of height, predicted jump length increases by about \(6.71\text{ cm}\).

options.label: B
options.text: For each extra \(1\text{ cm}\) jumped, height increases by \(6.71\text{ cm}\).

options.label: C
options.text: The predicted jump length starts at \(6.71\text{ cm}\).

subquestions.question_text: Find the wood production when rainfall is \(200\text{ mm}\).
Find the number of chairs produced when \(50\text{ kg}\) of wood is supplied.
If rainfall is \(500\text{ mm}\), how many chairs should Wooden Freaks expect to produce?
Will the May profit allow the company to buy a \(\$200000\) machine?

options.label: A
options.text: No, because the predicted profit is \(\$174875\), which is less than \(\$200000\).

options.label: B
options.text: Yes, because the predicted profit is \(\$200000\).

options.label: C
options.text: Yes, because the predicted profit is \(\$215000\).

options.label: D
options.text: No, because the predicted profit is \(\$7425\).

subquestions.question_text: Find the profit formula as a function of wood amount \(w\).
Find the range of the number of push-ups.
Find Pearson's correlation coefficient for height and push-ups.
Describe the relationship between height and push-ups.
Find the regression line for push-ups on height.
Estimate the number of push-ups for an athlete with height \(1.78\text{ m}\).
If the athlete actually did 45 push-ups, find the percentage error.
Find Pearson's correlation coefficient between Physics and Chemistry scores.
Evaluate the statement that students with higher Physics scores tend to have higher Chemistry scores.

options.label: A
options.text: The statement is contradicted by a strong negative correlation.

options.label: B
options.text: The statement is unsupported because \(r\) is close to 0.

options.label: C
options.text: The statement is supported by the strong positive correlation.

options.label: D
options.text: The statement proves Chemistry causes Physics scores to increase.

subquestions.question_text: Find the regression line of exam score on hours studied.
Interpret the slope \(2.44\) in context.

options.label: B
options.text: The predicted score when studying 0 hours is \(2.44\).

options.label: D
options.text: Each additional hour studied increases the predicted score by about \(2.44\) points.

subquestions.question_text: Use the regression line to predict the exam score for a student who studied 10 hours.
Is the prediction for 10 hours interpolation or extrapolation?

options.label: A
options.text: Extrapolation, because 10 is outside the observed range.

options.label: B
options.text: Interpolation, because 10 is the maximum value.

options.label: C
options.text: Interpolation, because 10 is within the observed range from 3 to 20 hours.

options.label: D
options.text: Extrapolation, because all predictions are extrapolations.

subquestions.question_text: Write down the correlation coefficient.
Describe the strength and direction of the correlation.
Which scatter diagram description matches the hours studied and exam score data?
Find Pearson's correlation coefficient.
Find the regression line of exam score on hours studied.
Estimate the exam score for a student who studies for \(6.5\) hours.
Comment on using the regression line for a student who studies 12 hours per week.

options.label: A
options.text: It is extrapolation and may not be reliable because 12 is outside the observed range.

options.label: B
options.text: It is interpolation and very reliable because 12 is inside the range.

options.label: C
options.text: It is invalid because Pearson's correlation is positive.

options.label: D
options.text: It is exact because regression always gives the true value.

subquestions.question_text: Find the mean warm-up time.
Find the mean number of calories burned.
Find the regression line of calories burned on warm-up time.
Estimate calories burned for an athlete who warms up for 14 minutes.
Find the range of the flight times.
Calculate Pearson's correlation coefficient for wing length and flight time.
Describe the correlation between wing length and flight time.
Find the regression line of flight time on wing length.
Estimate the flight time for a bird with wing length \(29\text{ cm}\).
If the actual flight time is \(9.9\text{ s}\), find the percentage error.
Is it valid to estimate flight time for a bird with wing length \(42\text{ cm}\)?

options.label: A
options.text: Yes, because regression is valid for all values.

options.label: B
options.text: Yes, because \(42\text{ cm}\) is close to the mean.

options.label: C
options.text: No, because the correlation is negative.

options.label: D
options.text: No, because \(42\text{ cm}\) is outside the observed wing-length range.

subquestions.question_text: State bird C's rank for wing length and rank for flight time, with rank 1 as the smallest value.
Describe the correlation between age and number of science documentaries watched.
Use \(y=0.32x-1.8\) to estimate the number of documentaries watched by a visitor aged 50.
Is the estimate for a 50-year-old visitor valid?

options.label: A
options.text: No, because 50 is outside the sampled age range.

options.label: B
options.text: No, because regression equations are never valid.

options.label: C
options.text: Yes, because 50 is the median age.

options.label: D
options.text: Yes, because 50 lies within the sampled age range.
